examples.explanation: Start: current='hit', path=['hit'], visited={'hit'}. Check neighbors: 'hot' (diff 1 char), add. Recursive call: current='hot', path=['hit','hot'], visited={'hit','hot'}. Check neighbors: 'dot' (diff 1), 'lot' (diff 1). Try 'dot': add. Recursive: current='dot', path=['hit','hot','dot'], visited={'hit','hot','dot'}. Check neighbors: 'dog' (diff 1). Try 'dog': add. Recursive: current='dog', path=['hit','hot','dot','dog'], visited={'hit','hot','dot','dog'}. Check neighbors: 'cog' (diff 1). Try 'cog': add. Recursive: current='cog', path=['hit','hot','dot','dog','cog'], visited update. Found endWord, path length 5, set min_len=5, add to results. Backtrack: remove 'cog', backtrack to 'dog', no more neighbors, backtrack to 'dot', no more, backtrack to 'hot'. Try 'lot': add. Recursive: current='lot', path=['hit','hot','lot'], visited={'hit','hot','lot'}. Check neighbors: 'log' (diff 1). Try 'log': add. Recursive: current='log', path=['hit','hot','lot','log'], visited update. Check neighbors: 'cog' (diff 1). Try 'cog': add. Recursive: current='cog', path=['hit','hot','lot','log','cog'], found endWord, length 5 equals min_len, add to results. Backtrack and finish. Return both sequences.

Start: current='a', path=['a'], visited={'a'}. Check neighbors: 'b' (diff 1 char), 'c' (diff 1). Try 'b': add. Recursive: current='b', path=['a','b'], visited={'a','b'}. Check neighbors: 'c' (diff 1). Try 'c': add. Recursive: current='c', path=['a','b','c'], found endWord, length 3, set min_len=3, add to results. Backtrack to 'a'. Try 'c': add. Recursive: current='c', path=['a','c'], found endWord, length 2 < min_len=3, clear results, set min_len=2, add ['a','c']. Backtrack and finish. Return single shortest sequence.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(b^d)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible sequences from beginWord to endWord using depth-first search, checking each word transformation by character changes. It is simple but impractical for large inputs due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS for All Shortest Word Ladders
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force DFS to find all shortest transformation sequences from beginWord to endWord.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using brute force DFS to find all shortest transformation sequences from beginWord to endWord. The intuition is to explore every possible path from beginWord to endWord using depth-first search, checking if each transformation changes exactly one character to a valid word in the wordList. We maintain a global list of all valid sequences and track the shortest length found so far to prune longer paths. The algorithm starts with beginWord in the current path, then recursively explores all possible next words that differ by exactly one character and are in wordList (or haven't been used in current path to avoid cycles). When we reach endWord, if the path length equals or is shorter than the current shortest, we add it to results (resetting if shorter). The time complexity is O(b^d) where b is branching factor and d is depth, as we explore all possible sequences. Space complexity is O(d) for recursion depth and path storage. This connects to L1 by using the DFS traversal as the main driver. Common mistakes include not pruning longer paths (leading to exponential blowup), not handling cycles properly, and not resetting results when shorter paths are found. Real code pattern: def findLadders(beginWord, endWord, wordList): results = []; min_len = float('inf'); def dfs(current, path, visited): nonlocal min_len; if current == endWord: if len(path) < min_len: results.clear(); min_len = len(path); if len(path) == min_len: results.append(path.copy()); return; for word in wordList: if word not in visited and one_char_diff(current, word): visited.add(word); path.append(word); dfs(word, path, visited); path.pop(); visited.remove(word); dfs(beginWord, [beginWord], {beginWord}); return results.

examples.trace: Start: current='hit', path=['hit'], visited={'hit'}. Check neighbors: 'hot' (diff 1 char), add. Recursive call: current='hot', path=['hit','hot'], visited={'hit','hot'}. Check neighbors: 'dot' (diff 1), 'lot' (diff 1). Try 'dot': add. Recursive: current='dot', path=['hit','hot','dot'], visited={'hit','hot','dot'}. Check neighbors: 'dog' (diff 1). Try 'dog': add. Recursive: current='dog', path=['hit','hot','dot','dog'], visited={'hit','hot','dot','dog'}. Check neighbors: 'cog' (diff 1). Try 'cog': add. Recursive: current='cog', path=['hit','hot','dot','dog','cog'], visited update. Found endWord, path length 5, set min_len=5, add to results. Backtrack: remove 'cog', backtrack to 'dog', no more neighbors, backtrack to 'dot', no more, backtrack to 'hot'. Try 'lot': add. Recursive: current='lot', path=['hit','hot','lot'], visited={'hit','hot','lot'}. Check neighbors: 'log' (diff 1). Try 'log': add. Recursive: current='log', path=['hit','hot','lot','log'], visited update. Check neighbors: 'cog' (diff 1). Try 'cog': add. Recursive: current='cog', path=['hit','hot','lot','log','cog'], found endWord, length 5 equals min_len, add to results. Backtrack and finish. Return both sequences.

Start: current='a', path=['a'], visited={'a'}. Check neighbors: 'b' (diff 1 char), 'c' (diff 1). Try 'b': add. Recursive: current='b', path=['a','b'], visited={'a','b'}. Check neighbors: 'c' (diff 1). Try 'c': add. Recursive: current='c', path=['a','b','c'], found endWord, length 3, set min_len=3, add to results. Backtrack to 'a'. Try 'c': add. Recursive: current='c', path=['a','c'], found endWord, length 2 < min_len=3, clear results, set min_len=2, add ['a','c']. Backtrack and finish. Return single shortest sequence.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal for Word Sequences
ladders.desc: Depth-first search function that explores all possible transformation sequences from current word to endWord.
ladders.explanation: This is the DFS traversal function that recursively explores all possible sequences from the current word to the endWord. The intuition is to systematically try every valid next word (differing by one character and in wordList) from the current position, building paths until we reach the endWord or exhaust options. The algorithm takes current word, current path list, and visited set as parameters. Base case: if current equals endWord, process the path (e.g., compare length to shortest found). Recursive case: iterate through wordList, for each word not in visited and differing by one character from current, add to visited and path, make recursive call, then backtrack by removing from path and visited. This ensures exploration of all permutations. Time complexity is O(b^d) in worst case where b is branching factor and d is depth, as it explores all paths. Space complexity is O(d) for recursion stack and path storage. This connects to L0 as the core recursive function that drives the full solution. Common mistakes include not backtracking properly (leading to incorrect visited states), not pruning based on path length, and infinite recursion if cycles aren't prevented. Real code pattern: def dfs(current, path, visited): if current == endWord: # process path; for word in wordList: if word not in visited and one_char_diff(current, word): visited.add(word); path.append(word); dfs(word, path, visited); path.pop(); visited.remove(word).

examples.trace: Start dfs('hot', ['hit','hot'], {'hit','hot'}). Not endWord. Check wordList: 'dot' (diff 1, not visited) → add to visited and path: visited={'hit','hot','dot'}, path=['hit','hot','dot']. Recursive call dfs('dot', path, visited). In that call: not endWord. Check neighbors: 'dog' (diff 1, not visited) → add: visited add 'dog', path append 'dog'. Recursive dfs('dog', path, visited). Found endWord, process path ['hit','hot','dot','dog']. Backtrack: remove 'dog' from path and visited. Back to 'dot', no more neighbors, backtrack: remove 'dot'. Back to 'hot'. Next: 'lot' (diff 1) → similar process if 'log' available. Finish.

Start dfs('a', ['a'], {'a'}). Not endWord. Check 'b': diff 1, add → dfs('b', ['a','b'], {'a','b'}). Not endWord. Check 'c': diff 1, add → dfs('c', ['a','b','c'], {'a','b','c'}). Found endWord, process path. Backtrack to 'a'. Check 'c': diff 1, add → dfs('c', ['a','c'], {'a','c'}). Found endWord, process path. Finish.

testCases.input: beginWord='hot', endWord='dog', wordList=['dot','dog'], current='hot', path=['hot'], visited={'hot'}
beginWord='hot', endWord='dog', wordList=['dot'], current='hot', path=['hot'], visited={'hot'}
beginWord='a', endWord='c', wordList=['b','c'], current='a', path=['a'], visited={'a'}
beginWord='red', endWord='tax', wordList=['ted','tex'], current='red', path=['red'], visited={'red'}
testCases.expected: No direct path, explores via 'ted' or 'tex'

testCases.input: beginWord='hot', endWord='hot', wordList=[], current='hot', path=['hot'], visited={'hot'}
beginWord='lost', endWord='cost', wordList=['most','cost'], current='lost', path=['lost'], visited={'lost'}
beginWord='game', endWord='thee', wordList=['fame'], current='game', path=['game'], visited={'game'}
beginWord='hit', endWord='cog', wordList=['hot'], current='hit', path=['hit'], visited={'hit'}
beginWord='a', endWord='d', wordList=['b','c','d'], current='a', path=['a'], visited={'a'}
beginWord='hot', endWord='dog', wordList=['dot','lot','dog'], current='hot', path=['hot'], visited={'hot'}

ladders.title: Core Logic — One-Character Difference Check
ladders.desc: Function to determine if two words differ by exactly one character.
ladders.explanation: This function checks if two words differ by exactly one character, which is the core transformation rule in word ladder problems. The intuition is that a valid transformation in the ladder requires changing only one character at a time, so we need to verify this condition efficiently. The algorithm compares two strings of equal length character by character, counting the number of positions where they differ. If the count is exactly 1, return True; otherwise, False. Step-by-step: initialize diff_count = 0; for i in range(len(word1)): if word1[i] != word2[i]: diff_count += 1; if diff_count > 1: return False early; after loop, return diff_count == 1. Time complexity is O(L) where L is word length, as we iterate through characters once. Space complexity is O(1) using only constant extra space. This connects to L1 as it's used in DFS to validate potential next words. Common mistakes include not handling words of different lengths (should return False), not early stopping when diff_count > 1, and off-by-one errors in counting. Real code pattern: def one_char_diff(word1, word2): if len(word1) != len(word2): return False; diff = 0; for i in range(len(word1)): if word1[i] != word2[i]: diff += 1; if diff > 1: return False; return diff == 1.

examples.trace: Lengths equal (3). Compare chars: i=0: 'h' vs 'h' → equal, diff=0. i=1: 'i' vs 'o' → not equal, diff=1. i=2: 't' vs 't' → equal, diff=1. End loop, diff=1 → return True.
Lengths equal (3). Compare: i=0: 'h' vs 'c' → diff=1. i=1: 'i' vs 'o' → diff=2. Since diff>1, early return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — List and Set Operations for Path Management
ladders.desc: Managing the current path and visited words using list and set data structures.
ladders.explanation: This involves using a list to track the current transformation path and a set to track visited words in the current path, which are essential for DFS traversal. The intuition is that we need to maintain the sequence of words (path) to build ladders and avoid revisiting words within the same path to prevent cycles. The list provides ordered storage for the path, allowing easy appending and popping (backtracking). The set provides O(1) average-time membership checks to quickly verify if a word has been visited. Algorithm: initialize path = [beginWord], visited = {beginWord}. When exploring a new word: add to visited set, append to path list. After recursive call: remove from path (pop last element), remove from visited. This ensures state is clean for exploring alternative branches. Time complexity: O(1) for add/remove operations on average. Space complexity: O(d) where d is path depth. This connects to L2 as these structures are used in DFS to manage state. Common mistakes include not using a set for visited (leading to O(n) checks), not backtracking properly (causing incorrect paths), and modifying collections incorrectly during iteration. Real code pattern: path = [start]; visited = {start}; def dfs(...): ... visited.add(word); path.append(word); dfs(...); path.pop(); visited.remove(word).

examples.output: After add: path=['hit','hot'], visited={'hit','hot'}. After backtrack: path=['hit'], visited={'hit'}.
examples.trace: Initial: path=['hit'], visited={'hit'}. To explore 'hot': visited.add('hot') → visited={'hit','hot'}; path.append('hot') → path=['hit','hot']. After recursive call: path.pop() removes 'hot' → path=['hit']; visited.remove('hot') → visited={'hit'}.

examples.input: Start with path=['a'], visited={'a'}. Explore 'b', then 'c'.
examples.output: Sequence: add 'b' → path=['a','b'], visited={'a','b'}; add 'c' → path=['a','b','c'], visited={'a','b','c'}; backtrack → path=['a','b'], visited={'a','b'}; backtrack → path=['a'], visited={'a'}.
examples.trace: Start: path=['a'], visited={'a'}. Add 'b': visited add 'b', path append 'b' → state1. Add 'c': visited add 'c', path append 'c' → state2. Backtrack 'c': path pop, visited remove 'c' → back to state1. Backtrack 'b': path pop, visited remove 'b' → back to initial.

testCases.input: path=['a','b','c'], visited={'a','b','c'}. Check if 'b' in visited.
path=['hit','hot','dot'], visited={'hit','hot','dot'}. Check if 'lot' in visited.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Comparing individual characters between two strings to find differences.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves comparing two strings character by character to determine if they are equal or count differences. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we need to examine each position to understand their relationship. The algorithm iterates through indices from 0 to length-1 (assuming equal lengths), accessing characters using indexing (e.g., word[i]) and comparing them with equality operator (==). For example, to count differences: initialize counter = 0; for i in range(len(word1)): if word1[i] != word2[i]: counter += 1. This basic operation is fundamental for checking one-character differences. Time complexity is O(L) where L is string length, as we examine each character once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used in the one-character difference check. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds if lengths differ (should check first), using incorrect comparison operators, and not iterating over all characters. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(str1)): if str1[i] == str2[i]: # equal case else: # not equal.

examples.output: Characters: c vs b (diff), a vs a (same), t vs t (same)
examples.trace: i=0: 'c' != 'b' → difference. i=1: 'a' == 'a' → equal. i=2: 't' == 't' → equal. Total differences: 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String as Sequence of Characters
ladders.desc: Understanding that a string is an ordered collection of characters that can be accessed by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding that a string is a sequence of characters, where each character has a position (index) starting from 0. The intuition is that strings are not just whole words but can be broken down into individual letters, which is essential for comparing and transforming words in word ladder problems. For example, the string 'cat' has characters: index 0: 'c', index 1: 'a', index 2: 't'. We can access these using square brackets: 'cat'[0] returns 'c'. Strings are immutable in many languages, meaning you cannot change a character directly, but you can create new strings. This concept is prerequisite for character-by-character comparison. Time/space: accessing by index is O(1) time, and strings occupy O(L) space where L is length. This connects to L4 as it enables character comparison. Common mistakes include confusing 0-based indexing (first character at index 0), trying to modify strings in-place, and not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: word = 'hello'; first_char = word[0] # 'h'; second_char = word[1] # 'e'; length = len(word) # 5.

examples.trace: The string 'dog' has 3 characters. Position 0: character 'd'. Position 1: character 'o'. Position 2: character 'g'. Access: 'dog'[0] gives 'd', 'dog'[1] gives 'o', 'dog'[2] gives 'g'.
String 'a' has length 1. The only character is at index 0: 'a'. Access: 'a'[0] returns 'a'.

approaches.name: BFS with Backtracking O(n * l * 26)
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search to find the shortest distance from beginWord to endWord, then backtrack from endWord to beginWord using a distance map to reconstruct all shortest paths. It efficiently avoids exploring longer paths.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS with Backtracking for All Shortest Word Ladders
ladders.desc: Complete implementation finding all shortest transformation sequences from beginWord to endWord using BFS distance mapping and DFS backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This solution combines BFS and backtracking to efficiently find all shortest transformation paths. The intuition is that BFS naturally finds the shortest distance from beginWord to endWord while building a distance map, then backtracking from endWord to beginWord using this map reconstructs only shortest paths without exploring longer ones. First, perform BFS starting from beginWord to compute the minimum distance to each word in wordList (including beginWord). Store distances in a dictionary. During BFS, also build an adjacency list of valid transformations. Then, perform DFS backtracking from endWord to beginWord, only moving to neighbors with distance exactly 1 less than current word. This ensures we only traverse shortest paths. Collect all valid sequences when reaching beginWord. Time complexity is O(n * l * 26) where n is number of words, l is word length - for each word, we generate all possible one-character variations. Space is O(n) for storing distances, adjacency list, and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This integrates the backtracking subroutine with BFS distance computation. Common mistakes include not handling cases where endWord is not in wordList, inefficiently building adjacency lists by comparing all word pairs (O(n²)), or forgetting to include beginWord in distance map. Real code pattern: 1) Add endWord to wordList if not present. 2) BFS from beginWord to compute distances. 3) DFS backtrack from endWord using distance map to collect paths. 4) Return all collected paths.

examples.trace: Step 1: BFS from 'hit'. Distances: hit:0, hot:1, dot:2, lot:2, dog:3, log:3, cog:4. Step 2: Backtrack from 'cog' (dist=4). Neighbors: 'dog'(3), 'log'(3). From 'dog'(3): neighbors 'dot'(2). From 'dot'(2): neighbors 'hot'(1). From 'hot'(1): neighbors 'hit'(0). Path: cog→dog→dot→hot→hit. From 'log'(3): neighbors 'lot'(2). From 'lot'(2): neighbors 'hot'(1). From 'hot'(1): neighbors 'hit'(0). Path: cog→log→lot→hot→hit. Collect both paths.

Step 1: BFS from 'red'. Distances: red:0, ted:1, rex:1, tad:2, tex:2, rax:2, tax:3. Step 2: Backtrack from 'tax'(3). Neighbors: 'tad'(2), 'tex'(2), 'rax'(2). From 'tad'(2): neighbors 'ted'(1). From 'ted'(1): neighbors 'red'(0). Path: tax→tad→ted→red. From 'tex'(2): neighbors 'ted'(1). Path: tax→tex→ted→red. From 'rax'(2): neighbors 'rex'(1). From 'rex'(1): neighbors 'red'(0). Path: tax→rax→rex→red. Collect all three paths.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking with Distance Map
ladders.desc: DFS backtracking from endWord to beginWord using precomputed distances to reconstruct only shortest paths.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine takes the endWord, beginWord, adjacency list, and distance map from BFS to find all shortest paths. The intuition is that by only moving to neighbors with distance exactly 1 less than current word, we guarantee we're following a shortest path backward. Start DFS at endWord with current path [endWord]. At each step, examine all neighbors of current word. For each neighbor, if its distance equals current distance minus 1, recursively continue DFS with that neighbor. When we reach beginWord (distance 0), add the reversed path to results (since we're backtracking from end to begin). Time complexity is O(P) where P is total number of nodes in all shortest paths - in worst case could be exponential but limited by shortest path constraint. Space is O(L) for recursion depth where L is path length. Connection to L0: This is the second half of the full solution, using BFS results. Connection to L2: This implements the core backtracking logic. Common mistakes include not reversing paths before adding to results, not checking distance condition correctly, or using visited set incorrectly (should allow revisiting in different paths). Real code pattern: def backtrack(word, path): if word == beginWord: results.append(path[::-1]); return. for neighbor in adjacency[word]: if distances[neighbor] == distances[word] - 1: backtrack(neighbor, path + [neighbor]).

examples.trace: Start at 'cog'(dist=4), path=['cog']. Neighbors: 'dog'(3), 'log'(3). Both have dist=3 (4-1). First 'dog': path=['cog','dog']. Neighbors: 'dot'(2). dist=2 equals 3-1. Path=['cog','dog','dot']. Neighbors: 'hot'(1). dist=1 equals 2-1. Path=['cog','dog','dot','hot']. Neighbors: 'hit'(0). dist=0 equals 1-1. Path=['cog','dog','dot','hot','hit']. Reverse: ['hit','hot','dot','dog','cog']. Second 'log': similar process yields ['hit','hot','lot','log','cog'].

Start at 'd'(dist=2), path=['d']. Neighbors: 'b'(1), 'c'(1). Both have dist=1 (2-1). First 'b': path=['d','b']. Neighbors: 'a'(0). dist=0 equals 1-1. Path=['d','b','a']. Reverse: ['a','b','d']. Second 'c': path=['d','c']. Neighbors: 'a'(0). Path=['d','c','a']. Reverse: ['a','c','d'].

ladders.title: Core Logic — BFS Distance Computation for Shortest Path
ladders.desc: Breadth-first search to compute minimum transformation distance from beginWord to all reachable words.
ladders.explanation: This core logic uses BFS to find the shortest distance from beginWord to every word in the graph. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level, so the first time we encounter a word, we've found the shortest path to it. Maintain a queue for BFS and a distance dictionary. Start with beginWord at distance 0. For each word dequeued, generate all possible one-character variations. For each variation that exists in wordList and hasn't been visited, set its distance to current distance + 1 and enqueue it. Continue until queue is empty or endWord is found (though we continue to find all distances). Time complexity is O(n * l * 26) where n is number of words, l is length - we process each word once and for each generate l*26 variations. Space is O(n) for queue and distance dictionary. Connection to L1: This produces the distance map needed for backtracking. Connection to L3: This uses the word variation generation building block. Common mistakes include not adding beginWord to distance map, using visited set incorrectly (should mark when enqueuing, not when processing), or stopping BFS too early (need distances for all words for backtracking). Real code pattern: distances = {beginWord: 0}; queue = deque([beginWord]); while queue: word = queue.popleft(); for i in range(len(word)): for c in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz': next_word = word[:i] + c + word[i+1:]; if next_word in wordSet and next_word not in distances: distances[next_word] = distances[word] + 1; queue.append(next_word).

examples.trace: Initialize: distances={'hit':0}, queue=['hit']. Dequeue 'hit'(dist=0). Generate variations: for position 0: 'ait','bit',...'hot' (found in wordList, not in distances). Add 'hot': dist=1, queue=['hot']. Position 1 variations: 'hat','hbt',...'hzt' (none in wordList). Position 2: 'hia','hib',...'hiz' (none). Dequeue 'hot'(dist=1). Variations: 'aot','bot',...'dot','lot' (both in wordList). Add 'dot': dist=2, 'lot': dist=2. Queue=['dot','lot']. Continue: 'dot' generates 'dog'(dist=3). 'lot' generates 'log'(dist=3). 'dog' generates 'cog'(dist=4). 'log' generates 'cog'(already found). Final distances as shown.

Start: distances={'red':0}, queue=['red']. Dequeue 'red'. Variations: position 0: 'aed','bed',...'ted' (found). Add 'ted': dist=1. Position 1: 'rad','rbd',...'rxd' (none). Position 2: 'rea','reb',...'rex' (found). Add 'rex': dist=1. Queue=['ted','rex']. 'ted' variations: 'red'(visited), 'tad'(dist=2), 'tex'(dist=2). 'rex' variations: 'red'(visited), 'rax'(dist=2). Queue=['tad','tex','rax']. 'tad' variations: 'ted'(visited), 'tax'(dist=3). 'tex' variations: 'ted'(visited), 'tax'(dist=3). 'rax' variations: 'rex'(visited), 'tax'(dist=3). Final distances.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All One-Character Variations
ladders.desc: For a given word, generate all possible words by changing exactly one character to any lowercase letter.
ladders.explanation: This building block creates all possible transformations of a word by altering exactly one character. The intuition is that in word ladder, valid moves are to words that differ by exactly one character, so generating all possibilities allows efficient neighbor finding. For a word of length l, there are l * 26 possible variations (26 letters for each position). We generate these by iterating through each position i from 0 to l-1, and for each, replace character at i with each lowercase letter 'a' to 'z', skipping the original character. Time complexity for one word is O(l * 26). Space is O(l * 26) for storing generated strings, though typically processed immediately. Connection to L2: This is used in BFS to find neighboring words. Connection to L4: This uses string manipulation operations. Common mistakes include generating variations that change multiple characters, not handling empty strings, or inefficiently checking all word pairs instead. Real code pattern: variations = []; for i in range(len(word)): for c in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz': if c != word[i]: new_word = word[:i] + c + word[i+1:]; variations.append(new_word).

examples.trace: Length=3. Position 0: replace 'h' with 'a'→'ait', 'b'→'bit', ... 'z'→'zit' (25 variations, skip 'h'). Position 1: replace 'i' with 'a'→'hat', 'b'→'hbt', ... 'z'→'hzt' (25 variations). Position 2: replace 't' with 'a'→'hia', 'b'→'hib', ... 'z'→'hiz' (25 variations). Total: 3*25=75 variations (actually 78 including original skipped ones).
Position 0: 'aog','bog',...,'cog',...,'zog' (25). Position 1: 'dag','dbg',...,'dzg' (25). Position 2: 'doa','dob',...,'doz' (25). Total 75 variations.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Manipulation for Word Transformation
ladders.desc: Replace a character at specific index in a string to create a new string.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation creates a new string by changing one character at a given position. The intuition is that strings are immutable in many languages, so we need to construct a new string rather than modify in place. For a string s of length n, to replace character at index i with character c, we take the substring before i (s[0:i]), add the new character c, then add the substring after i (s[i+1:]). This creates a new string with the modification. Time complexity is O(n) for slicing and concatenation. Space is O(n) for the new string. Connection to L3: This is used repeatedly in generating all variations. Connection to L5: This builds on string indexing and slicing concepts. Common mistakes include using mutable operations on strings, off-by-one errors in slicing, or not handling edge cases like empty strings or invalid indices. Real code pattern: new_string = s[:i] + c + s[i+1:]. For example, with s='word', i=1, c='a': s[:1]='w', s[2:]='rd', result='ward'.

examples.trace: s[:1] = 'h' (characters from index 0 to 0). s[2:] = 'llo' (from index 2 to end). Concatenate: 'h' + 'a' + 'llo' = 'hallo'.
s[:3] = 'tes' (indices 0-2). s[4:] = '' (empty since index 4 is beyond length). 'tes' + 'z' + '' = 'tesz'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Accessing individual characters and substrings in a string using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding how strings are indexed and how to extract parts of them. In programming, strings are sequences of characters where each character has an index starting from 0. For a string s of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. s[i] accesses the character at index i. Slicing s[start:end] extracts characters from index start up to but not including end. If start is omitted, it defaults to 0; if end is omitted, it defaults to length. Negative indices count from the end. The intuition is that strings are like arrays of characters, and slicing creates new strings. Time complexity for indexing is O(1), for slicing O(k) where k is length of slice. Space for slicing is O(k). Connection to L4: This enables the string manipulation operation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors with slicing bounds, confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based, or trying to modify strings directly (they're immutable). Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; s[0]='h'; s[1:4]='ell'; s[:3]='hel'; s[2:]='llo'.

examples.input: s='algorithm', access s[0], s[3], s[1:4], s[:5], s[6:]
examples.trace: s[0] is first character: 'a'. s[3] is fourth character (index 3): 'o'. s[1:4] extracts indices 1,2,3: 'l','g','o' → 'lgo'. s[:5] extracts indices 0-4: 'a','l','g','o','r' → 'algor'. s[6:] extracts from index 6 to end: 't','h','m' → 'thm'.

s[-1] is last character: 'a'. s[-2] is second last: 't'. s[1:-1] extracts from index 1 to second last: indices 1,2: 'a','t' → 'at'. s[:-2] extracts from start to third last: indices 0,1: 'd','a' → 'da'.
